can you explain why this:

with conversations as (select
    intercom_conversations.id,
    timestamp 'epoch' + intercom_conversations.created_at * interval '1 second' AS created_at,
    intercom_teams.name AS inbox_name, 
    intercom_admins.name AS admin_name, 
    intercom_conversations.statistics.time_to_admin_reply::integer,
    intercom_conversations.statistics.time_to_first_close::integer,
    intercom_conversations.state = 'closed' AS is_closed,
    -- if the user who closed the conversation was 6649497 (our Intercom account's bot user) we know it was auto-closed
    COALESCE(intercom_conversations.statistics.last_closed_by_id::varchar = '6649497', FALSE) AS was_auto_closed,
    row_number() over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) as rn,
    (least(1, sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'assignment' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id)) +
    sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type in ('admin', 'user') then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) + 1)::float AS conversation_length,
    
    (least(1, sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'assignment' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id)) +
    sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type = 'admin' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id))::float AS conversation_admin_length,
    
    (sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type = 'user' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) + 1)::float AS conversation_user_length,
    
    intercom_conversations.conversation_rating.rating::integer,
    
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text AS tag_1,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text AS tag_2,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text AS tag_3,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text AS tag_4,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text AS tag_5,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text AS tag_6
from intercom_conversations
left join intercom_teams on intercom_teams.id = intercom_conversations.team_assignee_id
left join intercom_admins on intercom_admins.id = intercom_conversations.admin_assignee_id
left join intercom_conversation_parts on intercom_conversation_parts.conversation_id = intercom_conversations.id
where intercom_conversations.custom_attributes.["Ticket category"] is null
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting'))
select *
from conversations
where rn = 1

union

select
 conversation_id,
 created_at::timestamp,
 CASE 
   WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '245420_hiver/Anna_Rhatigan_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Anna'
   WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '191527_hiver/Priority_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Priority'
   ELSE SPLIT_PART(SPLIT_PART(_ab_source_file_url, '/', 2), '_', 1)
 END AS inbox_name,
   CASE WHEN first_responder = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_responder END AS admin_name, 
    CASE WHEN first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_::float END AS time_to_admin_reply,
    CASE WHEN resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_::float END AS time_to_first_close,
    case when (resolved_at <> 'NA' and resolved_at is not null) or (status = 'Closed' and conversation_type <> 'Outbound') then true else false end AS is_closed,
    FALSE as was_auto_closed,
    1 AS rn,
    total_emails_count::float AS conversation_length,
    emails_sent_count::float AS conversation_admin_length,
    total_emails_count::float - emails_sent_count::float AS conversation_user_length,
    CASE WHEN csat_rating = 'Not satisfied' THEN 1 WHEN csat_rating = 'Okay' THEN 3 WHEN csat_rating = 'Satisfied' THEN 5 ELSE NULL END AS rating,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 1) END AS tag_1,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 2) END AS tag_2,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 3) END AS tag_3,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 4) END AS tag_4,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 5) END AS tag_5,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 6) END AS tag_6
from hiver_new_conversations

is giving a different result than @views/standard_data_refresh/08_intercom_conversations_with_topics.sql 

i am trying to get the latter closer to the former

content.text:
Looking at these two queries, they're pulling completely different data:

1. **Your query** combines:
   - `intercom_conversations` table with conversation-level metrics
   - `hiver_new_conversations` table (appears to be imported CSV data from Hiver)
   - Focuses on conversation statistics, ratings, tags, and admin/team assignments

2. **The file (08_intercom_conversations_with_topics.sql)** uses:
   - `intercom_conversation_parts` table (individual messages)
   - Focuses on message-level data with topic detection (1583, verification, bot spam)

They serve different purposes:
- **Your query**: Conversation-level analytics (response times, ratings, assignments)
- **The file**: Message-level topic analysis (what customers are talking about)

To make the file query more like yours, you'd need to:
1. Switch from part-level to conversation-level aggregation
2. Add conversation statistics fields
3. Include team/admin assignments
4. Add conversation ratings and tags

Would you like me to modify the topics query to include conversation-level metrics similar to your query?

This makes the query capture more conversation data, but it's still focused on individual messages rather than conversation-level aggregates like your first query.

The key difference is:
- **Your first query**: One row per **conversation** with aggregated statistics
- **PR #136 and current file**: One row per **message/part** within conversations

To make the `08_intercom_conversations_with_topics.sql` file align with your conversation-level query, we'd need to transform it from a message-level to conversation-level view. Would you like me to:

1. **Keep the message-level granularity** but add conversation statistics as additional columns (each message would have the same conversation stats repeated)?
2. **Change to conversation-level granularity** (one row per conversation) with topic flags aggregated across all messages in that conversation?
3. **Create a new view** that's conversation-level while keeping the existing message-level view?

Option 2 seems most aligned with your first query - it would give you one row per conversation with statistics, topics detected anywhere in the conversation, ratings, tags, etc. Is that what you're looking for?

/*
================================================================================
TABLE: view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
<<<<<<< HEAD:views/intercom_conversations_with_topics.sql

NOTE: Despite the "view_" prefix, this is actually a TABLE, not a VIEW. We use the "view_"
prefix to keep it sorted alongside our other view_* objects. This must be a table (not a view)
to avoid DROP CASCADE issues when materialized views are refreshed.

This table tracks Intercom conversation parts AND initial conversation messages to identify customer service topics.
Used in Metabase to monitor conversations about 1583 forms, verification, and account types.
Each row represents a single conversation part (message) or initial email with topic detection.

KEY FEATURES:
- Includes BOTH conversation parts (replies) AND initial conversation messages (first emails)
- Identifies bot spam messages for filtering
- Flags conversations mentioning 1583, verification, primary, or secondary accounts
- Converts Unix timestamps to readable dates
- Extracts author information from JSON fields

OUTPUT COLUMNS:
- part_id: Unique identifier for the conversation part (or 'conv_[id]' for initial messages)
- body: The message content (from either conversation_parts.body or conversation_message.body)
- author_type: Type of author (user, admin, bot, etc.)
- author_email: Email address of the author
- conversation_id: ID linking to parent conversation
- part_created_date: When this message was created
- conversation_created_date: When the conversation started
- bot_spam: Boolean flag for automated bot messages
- mentions_1583: Boolean flag for 1583-related topics
================================================================================
*/

-- Step 1: Drop the _new table if it exists (cleanup from any previous failed runs)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new;

-- Step 2: Create the new table with fresh data
CREATE TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new
DISTKEY(conversation_id)
SORTKEY(conversation_created_date, part_created_date)
AS
WITH all_parts AS (
  -- Include all comment parts from conversation_parts
  SELECT
    cp.id AS part_id,
    cp.body,
    cp.author,
    cp.conversation_id,
    cp.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
  JOIN public.intercom_conversations c ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT)
  WHERE cp.part_type = 'comment'

  UNION ALL

  -- Include initial conversation messages for conversations without comment parts
  SELECT
    CONCAT('conv_', c.id) AS part_id,  -- Create unique ID for initial message
    c.conversation_message.body::VARCHAR AS body,
    c.conversation_message.author AS author,
    CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) AS conversation_id,
    c.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversations c
  WHERE c.conversation_message.body IS NOT NULL
    AND c.conversation_message.body != ''
    AND c.conversation_message.body != '{}'
    -- Only include conversations that have no comment parts (to avoid duplicates)
    AND NOT EXISTS (
      SELECT 1 FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp2 
      WHERE cp2.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
      AND cp2.part_type = 'comment'
    )
)
SELECT
  part_id,
  body,
  REPLACE(author.type::VARCHAR, '"', '') AS author_type,
  REPLACE(author.email::VARCHAR, '"', '') AS author_email,
  conversation_id::VARCHAR AS conversation_id,
  (TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + part_created_at * INTERVAL '1 second')::TIMESTAMP AS part_created_date,
  (TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + conversation_created_at * INTERVAL '1 second')::TIMESTAMP AS conversation_created_date,
  -- Flag for automated bot messages to filter out spam
  CASE
    WHEN body ILIKE '%unmonitored inbox%' THEN TRUE
    ELSE FALSE
  END AS bot_spam,
  -- Flag conversations mentioning 1583, verification, primary, or secondary accounts
  -- Also checks for specific Intercom tags: t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue and t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(body) ILIKE '%1583%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%verification%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%primary%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%secondary%'
    OR JSON_SERIALIZE(tags) ILIKE '%t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue%'
    OR JSON_SERIALIZE(tags) ILIKE '%t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay%' THEN TRUE
    ELSE FALSE
  END AS mentions_1583
FROM all_parts;

-- Step 3: Atomic swap - drop old and rename new
BEGIN;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;
ALTER TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new RENAME TO view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;

COMMIT;

-- Step 4: Add comprehensive comment for Claude discoverability and documentation
COMMENT ON TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics IS '
================================================================================
TABLE: view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
=======
>>>>>>> origin/master:views/standard_data_refresh/08_intercom_conversations_with_topics.sql
PURPOSE: Track and analyze Intercom conversation topics for customer service insights
================================================================================

DESCRIPTION:
This table processes Intercom conversation data to identify key customer service topics
and patterns. Each row represents a single conversation part (message) with automated
topic detection for 1583 forms, verification processes, and account types.

USE CASES:
- Monitor customer inquiries about 1583 tax forms
- Track verification-related support requests
- Analyze primary vs secondary account questions
- Filter out bot spam for cleaner analytics
- Customer service topic trending in Metabase dashboards

KEY COLUMNS:
- part_id: Unique identifier for each conversation part
- body: The actual message text content
- author_type: Classification of who sent the message (user/admin/bot)
- author_email: Email address of the message sender
- conversation_id: Links to parent conversation for threading
- part_created_date: Timestamp when this specific message was created
- conversation_created_date: Timestamp when the overall conversation started
- bot_spam: TRUE if message contains "unmonitored inbox" (automated messages)
- mentions_1583: TRUE if message mentions 1583, verification, primary, or secondary

TOPIC DETECTION LOGIC:

  - Triggered by: "%unmonitored inbox%" (case-insensitive)

- mentions_1583: TRUE if message mentions 1583, verification, primary, or secondary

bot_spam flag:

  - Purpose: Filter automated/system messages from analysis

mentions_1583 flag:
  - Triggered by message content: "%1583%", "%verification%", "%primary%", "%secondary%"
  - Triggered by Intercom tags: "t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue", "t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay"
  - All matches are case-insensitive using ILIKE
  - Helps identify tax form and account-related inquiries from both message content and support team tagging

DATA SOURCES:
- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Individual messages within conversations
- public.intercom_conversations: Parent conversation metadata

DATA TRANSFORMATIONS:
- Includes BOTH conversation parts (replies) AND initial conversation messages (first emails)
- Unix timestamps converted to TIMESTAMP format using epoch calculation
- JSON author fields extracted and cleaned (removes quotes)
- Only 'comment' type parts included (excludes system messages)
- Initial conversation messages included for conversations without comment parts

FILTERING:
- Only includes conversation parts where part_type = 'comment'
- Initial messages only included if conversation has no comment parts (avoids duplicates)
- This excludes system-generated parts like assignments, notes, etc.

REFRESH STRATEGY:
- Uses atomic swap pattern (create new, swap, drop old)
- Table despite view_ prefix to avoid CASCADE dependency issues
- DISTKEY on conversation_id for optimal join performance
- SORTKEY on dates for efficient time-based queries

- Distributed by conversation_id for efficient joins
- Sorted by creation dates for time-series analysis
- Table format provides faster query performance than view

ORDER BY part_created_date DESC;

  COUNT(DISTINCT conversation_id) as total_conversations
FROM view_intercom_conversations_with_topics

ORDER BY 1 DESC;

DISTKEY(conversation_id)
SORTKEY(conversation_created_date, part_created_date)
AS
WITH all_parts AS (
  -- Include all comment parts from conversation_parts
  SELECT
    cp.id AS part_id,
    cp.body,
    cp.author,
    cp.conversation_id,
    cp.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
  JOIN public.intercom_conversations c ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT)
  WHERE cp.part_type = 'comment'

  UNION ALL

  -- Include initial conversation messages for conversations without comment parts
  SELECT
    CONCAT('conv_', c.id) AS part_id,  -- Create unique ID for initial message
    c.conversation_message.body::VARCHAR AS body,
    c.conversation_message.author AS author,
    CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) AS conversation_id,
    c.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversations c
  WHERE c.conversation_message.body IS NOT NULL
    AND c.conversation_message.body != ''
    AND c.conversation_message.body != '{}'

      WHERE cp2.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
      AND cp2.part_type = 'comment'
    )
)

  -- Flag for automated bot messages to filter out spam

    WHEN body ILIKE '%unmonitored inbox%' THEN TRUE
    ELSE FALSE
  END AS bot_spam,
  -- Flag conversations mentioning 1583, verification, primary, or secondary accounts
  -- Also checks for specific Intercom tags: t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue and t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(body) ILIKE '%1583%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%verification%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%primary%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%secondary%'
    OR JSON_SERIALIZE(tags) ILIKE '%t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue%'

    ELSE FALSE

FROM all_parts;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;
ALTER TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new RENAME TO view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;

COMMIT;

content.text: I found the issue! The conversations without comment parts all have NULL bodies in their initial messages, so they're being filtered out by our current logic. Let me check what your original query approach would capture:

input.sql_query:
WITH conversations AS (
  SELECT
    intercom_conversations.id,
    timestamp 'epoch' + intercom_conversations.created_at * interval '1 second' AS created_at,
    intercom_teams.name AS inbox_name, 
    intercom_admins.name AS admin_name,
    row_number() over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) as rn
  FROM intercom_conversations
  LEFT JOIN intercom_teams ON intercom_teams.id = intercom_conversations.team_assignee_id
  LEFT JOIN intercom_admins ON intercom_admins.id = intercom_conversations.admin_assignee_id
  LEFT JOIN intercom_conversation_parts ON intercom_conversation_parts.conversation_id = intercom_conversations.id
  WHERE intercom_conversations.custom_attributes.["Ticket category"] IS NULL
    AND (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text IS NULL OR intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
)
SELECT 
  'Original query approach' as approach,
  COUNT(DISTINCT id) as unique_conversations
FROM conversations

toolUseResult.originalFile:
/*
================================================================================
TABLE: view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
PURPOSE: Track and analyze Intercom conversation topics for customer service insights
================================================================================

DESCRIPTION:
This table processes Intercom conversation data to identify key customer service topics
and patterns. Each row represents a single conversation part (message) OR initial 
conversation message with automated topic detection for 1583 forms, verification 
processes, and account types.

USE CASES:
- Monitor customer inquiries about 1583 tax forms
- Track verification-related support requests
- Analyze primary vs secondary account questions
- Filter out bot spam for cleaner analytics
- Customer service topic trending in Metabase dashboards

KEY COLUMNS:
- part_id: Unique identifier for each conversation part (or 'conv_[id]' for initial messages)
- body: The actual message text content (from either conversation_parts.body or conversation_message.body)
- author_type: Classification of who sent the message (user/admin/bot)
- author_email: Email address of the message sender
- conversation_id: Links to parent conversation for threading
- part_created_date: Timestamp when this specific message was created
- conversation_created_date: Timestamp when the overall conversation started
- bot_spam: TRUE if message contains "unmonitored inbox" (automated messages)
- mentions_1583: TRUE if message mentions 1583, verification, primary, or secondary

TOPIC DETECTION LOGIC:
bot_spam flag:
  - Triggered by: "%unmonitored inbox%" (case-insensitive)
  - Purpose: Filter automated/system messages from analysis

mentions_1583 flag:
  - Triggered by message content: "%1583%", "%verification%", "%primary%", "%secondary%"
  - Triggered by Intercom tags: "t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue", "t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay"
  - All matches are case-insensitive using ILIKE
  - Helps identify tax form and account-related inquiries from both message content and support team tagging

DATA SOURCES:
- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Individual messages within conversations
- public.intercom_conversations: Parent conversation metadata

DATA TRANSFORMATIONS:
- Includes BOTH conversation parts (replies) AND initial conversation messages (first emails)
- Unix timestamps converted to TIMESTAMP format using epoch calculation
- JSON author fields extracted and cleaned (removes quotes)
- Only 'comment' type parts included (excludes system messages)
- Initial conversation messages included for conversations without comment parts

FILTERING:
- Only includes conversation parts where part_type = 'comment'
- Initial messages only included if conversation has no comment parts (avoids duplicates)
- This excludes system-generated parts like assignments, notes, etc.

REFRESH STRATEGY:
- Uses atomic swap pattern (create new, swap, drop old)
- Table despite view_ prefix to avoid CASCADE dependency issues
- DISTKEY on conversation_id for optimal join performance
- SORTKEY on dates for efficient time-based queries

PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS:
- Distributed by conversation_id for efficient joins
- Sorted by creation dates for time-series analysis
- Table format provides faster query performance than view

USAGE EXAMPLES:
-- Find all 1583-related conversations in the last 30 days
SELECT conversation_id, part_created_date, body
FROM view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
WHERE mentions_1583 = TRUE
  AND part_created_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 30
  AND bot_spam = FALSE
ORDER BY part_created_date DESC;

-- Count daily conversation topics
SELECT
  DATE(part_created_date) as date,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN mentions_1583 THEN 1 END) as count_1583_mentions,
  COUNT(DISTINCT conversation_id) as total_conversations
FROM view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
WHERE bot_spam = FALSE
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1 DESC;

-- Analyze author types for 1583 conversations
SELECT
  author_type,
  COUNT(*) as message_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT conversation_id) as conversation_count
FROM view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
WHERE mentions_1583 = TRUE
GROUP BY 1;

RELATED OBJECTS:
- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Source table for message data
- public.intercom_conversations: Source table for conversation metadata
- Metabase dashboards: Primary consumer of this data

LAST MODIFIED: 2025-10-23
OWNER: Data Team

NOTE: Despite the "view_" prefix, this is actually a TABLE, not a VIEW. We use the "view_"
prefix to keep it sorted alongside our other view_* objects. This must be a table (not a view)
to avoid DROP CASCADE issues when materialized views are refreshed.
================================================================================
*/

-- Step 1: Drop the _new table if it exists (cleanup from any previous failed runs)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new;

-- Step 2: Create the new table with fresh data
CREATE TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new
DISTKEY(conversation_id)
SORTKEY(conversation_created_date, part_created_date)
AS
WITH all_parts AS (
  -- Include all comment parts from conversation_parts
  SELECT
    cp.id AS part_id,
    cp.body,
    cp.author,
    cp.conversation_id,
    cp.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
  JOIN public.intercom_conversations c ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT)
  WHERE cp.part_type = 'comment'

  UNION ALL

  -- Include initial conversation messages for conversations without comment parts
  SELECT
    CONCAT('conv_', c.id) AS part_id,  -- Create unique ID for initial message
    c.conversation_message.body::VARCHAR AS body,
    c.conversation_message.author AS author,
    CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) AS conversation_id,
    c.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags
  FROM public.intercom_conversations c
  WHERE c.conversation_message.body IS NOT NULL
    AND c.conversation_message.body != ''
    AND c.conversation_message.body != '{}'
    -- Only include conversations that have no comment parts (to avoid duplicates)
    AND NOT EXISTS (
      SELECT 1 FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp2 
      WHERE cp2.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
      AND cp2.part_type = 'comment'
    )
)
SELECT
  part_id,
  body,
  REPLACE(author.type::VARCHAR, '"', '') AS author_type,
  REPLACE(author.email::VARCHAR, '"', '') AS author_email,
  conversation_id::VARCHAR AS conversation_id,
  (TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + part_created_at * INTERVAL '1 second')::TIMESTAMP AS part_created_date,
  (TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + conversation_created_at * INTERVAL '1 second')::TIMESTAMP AS conversation_created_date,
  -- Flag for automated bot messages to filter out spam
  CASE
    WHEN body ILIKE '%unmonitored inbox%' THEN TRUE
    ELSE FALSE
  END AS bot_spam,
  -- Flag conversations mentioning 1583, verification, primary, or secondary accounts
  -- Also checks for specific Intercom tags: t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue and t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(body) ILIKE '%1583%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%verification%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%primary%'
    OR LOWER(body) ILIKE '%secondary%'
    OR JSON_SERIALIZE(tags) ILIKE '%t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue%'
    OR JSON_SERIALIZE(tags) ILIKE '%t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay%' THEN TRUE
    ELSE FALSE
  END AS mentions_1583
FROM all_parts;

-- Step 3: Atomic swap - drop old and rename new
BEGIN;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;
ALTER TABLE view_intercom_conversations_with_topics_new RENAME TO view_intercom_conversations_with_topics;

COMMIT;

+/*
+================================================================================
+TABLE: view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
+PURPOSE: Track and analyze Intercom conversation topics for customer service insights
+================================================================================
+
+DESCRIPTION:
+This table processes Intercom conversation data to identify key customer service topics
+and patterns. Each row represents a single conversation part (message) OR initial 
+conversation message with automated topic detection for 1583 forms, verification 
+processes, and account types.
+
+USE CASES:
+- Monitor customer inquiries about 1583 tax forms
+- Track verification-related support requests
+- Analyze primary vs secondary account questions
+- Filter out bot spam for cleaner analytics
+- Customer service topic trending in Metabase dashboards
+
+KEY COLUMNS:
+- part_id: Unique identifier for each conversation part (or 'conv_[id]' for initial messages)
+- body: The actual message text content (from either conversation_parts.body or conversation_message.body)
+- author_type: Classification of who sent the message (user/admin/bot)
+- author_email: Email address of the message sender
+- conversation_id: Links to parent conversation for threading
+- part_created_date: Timestamp when this specific message was created
+- conversation_created_date: Timestamp when the overall conversation started
+- bot_spam: TRUE if message contains "unmonitored inbox" (automated messages)
+- mentions_1583: TRUE if message mentions 1583, verification, primary, or secondary
+
+TOPIC DETECTION LOGIC:
+bot_spam flag:
+  - Triggered by: "%unmonitored inbox%" (case-insensitive)
+  - Purpose: Filter automated/system messages from analysis
+
+mentions_1583 flag:
+  - Triggered by message content: "%1583%", "%verification%", "%primary%", "%secondary%"
+  - Triggered by Intercom tags: "t-Cat-Onboarding-1583-Issue", "t-Cat-Onboarding-Verification-Delay"
+  - All matches are case-insensitive using ILIKE
+  - Helps identify tax form and account-related inquiries from both message content and support team tagging
+
+DATA SOURCES:
+- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Individual messages within conversations
+- public.intercom_conversations: Parent conversation metadata
+
+DATA TRANSFORMATIONS:
+- Includes BOTH conversation parts (replies) AND initial conversation messages (first emails)
+- Unix timestamps converted to TIMESTAMP format using epoch calculation
+- JSON author fields extracted and cleaned (removes quotes)
+- Only 'comment' type parts included (excludes system messages)
+- Initial conversation messages included for conversations without comment parts
+
+FILTERING:
+- Only includes conversation parts where part_type = 'comment'
+- Initial messages only included if conversation has no comment parts (avoids duplicates)
+- This excludes system-generated parts like assignments, notes, etc.
+
+REFRESH STRATEGY:
+- Uses atomic swap pattern (create new, swap, drop old)
+- Table despite view_ prefix to avoid CASCADE dependency issues
+- DISTKEY on conversation_id for optimal join performance
+- SORTKEY on dates for efficient time-based queries
+
+PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS:
+- Distributed by conversation_id for efficient joins
+- Sorted by creation dates for time-series analysis
+- Table format provides faster query performance than view
+
+USAGE EXAMPLES:
+-- Find all 1583-related conversations in the last 30 days
+SELECT conversation_id, part_created_date, body
+FROM view_intercom_conversations_with_topics
+WHERE mentions_1583 = TRUE
+  AND part_created_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 30

WITH all_parts AS (
  -- Include all comment parts from conversation_parts
  SELECT
    cp.id AS part_id,
    cp.body,
    cp.author,
    cp.conversation_id,
    cp.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags,
    c.team_assignee_id,
    c.admin_assignee_id,
    c.statistics,
    c.state,
    c.conversation_rating
  FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
  JOIN public.intercom_conversations c ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT)
  WHERE cp.part_type = 'comment'

  UNION ALL

  -- Include initial conversation messages (or placeholder) for conversations without comment parts
  -- This ensures ALL conversations are represented, even those with no messages
  SELECT
    CONCAT('conv_', c.id) AS part_id,  -- Create unique ID for initial message
    COALESCE(c.conversation_message.body::VARCHAR, '') AS body,  -- Use empty string if NULL
    c.conversation_message.author AS author,
    CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) AS conversation_id,
    c.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags,
    c.team_assignee_id,
    c.admin_assignee_id,
    c.statistics,
    c.state,
    c.conversation_rating
  FROM public.intercom_conversations c
  WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp2 
    WHERE cp2.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
    AND cp2.part_type = 'comment'
  )
)
SELECT
  part_id,

with conversations as (select
    intercom_conversations.id,
    timestamp 'epoch' + intercom_conversations.created_at * interval '1 second' AS created_at,
    intercom_teams.name AS inbox_name, 
    intercom_admins.name AS admin_name, 
    intercom_conversations.statistics.time_to_admin_reply::integer,
    intercom_conversations.statistics.time_to_first_close::integer,
    intercom_conversations.state = 'closed' AS is_closed,
    -- if the user who closed the conversation was 6649497 (our Intercom account's bot user) we know it was auto-closed
    COALESCE(intercom_conversations.statistics.last_closed_by_id::varchar = '6649497', FALSE) AS was_auto_closed,
    row_number() over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) as rn,
    (least(1, sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'assignment' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id)) +
    sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type in ('admin', 'user') then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) + 1)::float AS conversation_length,
    
    (least(1, sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'assignment' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id)) +
    sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type = 'admin' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id))::float AS conversation_admin_length,
    
    (sum(case when intercom_conversation_parts.part_type = 'comment' and intercom_conversation_parts.author.type = 'user' then 1 else 0 end) over (partition by intercom_conversations.id) + 1)::float AS conversation_user_length,
    
    intercom_conversations.conversation_rating.rating::integer,
    
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text AS tag_1,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text AS tag_2,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text AS tag_3,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text AS tag_4,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text AS tag_5,
    intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text AS tag_6
from intercom_conversations
left join intercom_teams on intercom_teams.id = intercom_conversations.team_assignee_id
left join intercom_admins on intercom_admins.id = intercom_conversations.admin_assignee_id
left join intercom_conversation_parts on intercom_conversation_parts.conversation_id = intercom_conversations.id
where intercom_conversations.custom_attributes.["Ticket category"] is null
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[0].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[1].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[2].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[3].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[4].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
and (intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text is null or intercom_conversations.tags.tags[5].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting'))
select *
from conversations
where rn = 1

union

select
 conversation_id,
 created_at::timestamp,
 CASE 
   WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '245420_hiver/Anna_Rhatigan_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Anna'
   WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '191527_hiver/Priority_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Priority'
   ELSE SPLIT_PART(SPLIT_PART(_ab_source_file_url, '/', 2), '_', 1)
 END AS inbox_name,
   CASE WHEN first_responder = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_responder END AS admin_name, 
    CASE WHEN first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_::float END AS time_to_admin_reply,
    CASE WHEN resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_::float END AS time_to_first_close,
    case when (resolved_at <> 'NA' and resolved_at is not null) or (status = 'Closed' and conversation_type <> 'Outbound') then true else false end AS is_closed,
    FALSE as was_auto_closed,
    1 AS rn,
    total_emails_count::float AS conversation_length,
    emails_sent_count::float AS conversation_admin_length,
    total_emails_count::float - emails_sent_count::float AS conversation_user_length,
    CASE WHEN csat_rating = 'Not satisfied' THEN 1 WHEN csat_rating = 'Okay' THEN 3 WHEN csat_rating = 'Satisfied' THEN 5 ELSE NULL END AS rating,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 1) END AS tag_1,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 2) END AS tag_2,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 3) END AS tag_3,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 4) END AS tag_4,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 5) END AS tag_5,
    CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 6) END AS tag_6
from hiver_new_conversations

input.content:
/*
================================================================================
TABLE: view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations
PURPOSE: Unified view of all customer conversations from both Intercom and Hiver
================================================================================

DESCRIPTION:
This table provides a complete view of customer conversations across both Intercom 
(current system) and Hiver (legacy system used until April 2025). When we migrated 
from Hiver to Intercom, conversations were imported but lost critical metadata. 
This view reconstructs the complete picture by:
- Excluding Hiver-imported conversations from Intercom (tagged 'ignore-for-reporting')
- Including original Hiver data with full metadata preserved
- Providing unified conversation-level metrics across both platforms

USE CASES:
- Historical reporting spanning both support platforms
- Customer service metrics and KPIs
- Response time and resolution analysis
- CSAT tracking across the platform transition
- Team and admin performance metrics

KEY COLUMNS:
- id: Conversation identifier
- created_at: When the conversation started
- inbox_name: Team/inbox assignment
- admin_name: Assigned support agent
- time_to_admin_reply: Response time in seconds
- time_to_first_close: Resolution time in seconds
- is_closed: Whether conversation is resolved
- was_auto_closed: If closed by automation (bot ID: 6649497)
- conversation_length: Total number of messages
- conversation_admin_length: Number of admin messages
- conversation_user_length: Number of customer messages
- rating: CSAT score (1=Not satisfied, 3=Okay, 5=Satisfied)
- tag_1 through tag_6: Conversation tags

DATA SOURCES:
- public.intercom_conversations: Current support platform (April 2025 - present)
- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Message details for length calculations
- public.hiver_new_conversations: Legacy platform data (through April 2025)
- public.intercom_teams: Team/inbox information
- public.intercom_admins: Support agent information

DEDUPLICATION STRATEGY:
Hiver conversations were imported into Intercom in April 2025 but tagged with 
'ignore-for-reporting' to prevent double-counting. This view:
1. Filters OUT imported Hiver data from Intercom side
2. Includes original Hiver data with complete metadata
3. Uses UNION to combine both sources without duplicates

FILTERING LOGIC:
- Excludes conversations with custom attribute "Ticket category" (likely test/internal)
- Excludes any conversation tagged 'ignore-for-reporting' from Intercom
- Uses row_number() to deduplicate when conversations have multiple parts

PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS:
- Distributed by conversation ID for optimal join performance
- Sorted by creation date for time-series queries
- Table format (not view) to avoid CASCADE issues with materialized views

USAGE EXAMPLES:
-- Get daily conversation metrics
SELECT 
  DATE(created_at) as date,
  COUNT(*) as total_conversations,
  AVG(time_to_admin_reply) as avg_response_time,
  AVG(CASE WHEN rating IS NOT NULL THEN rating END) as avg_csat
FROM view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1 DESC;

-- Analyze inbox workload
SELECT 
  inbox_name,
  COUNT(*) as conversation_count,
  AVG(conversation_length) as avg_length,
  SUM(CASE WHEN is_closed THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)::FLOAT / COUNT(*) as close_rate
FROM view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations
GROUP BY 1;

RELATED OBJECTS:
- view_intercom_conversations_with_topics: Message-level topic analysis
- Metabase dashboards: Primary consumer for support metrics

LAST MODIFIED: 2025-02-20
OWNER: Data Team
================================================================================
*/

-- Step 1: Drop the _new table if it exists (cleanup from any previous failed runs)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations_new;

-- Step 2: Create the new table with fresh data
CREATE TABLE view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations_new
DISTKEY(id)
SORTKEY(created_at)
AS
WITH intercom_conversations_filtered AS (
  SELECT
    ic.id,
    TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + ic.created_at * INTERVAL '1 second' AS created_at,
    it.name AS inbox_name, 
    ia.name AS admin_name, 
    ic.statistics.time_to_admin_reply::INTEGER AS time_to_admin_reply,
    ic.statistics.time_to_first_close::INTEGER AS time_to_first_close,
    ic.state = 'closed' AS is_closed,
    -- Bot user ID 6649497 indicates auto-closed
    COALESCE(ic.statistics.last_closed_by_id::VARCHAR = '6649497', FALSE) AS was_auto_closed,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id) AS rn,
    -- Calculate conversation length including initial message
    (LEAST(1, SUM(CASE WHEN cp.part_type = 'assignment' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id)) +
     SUM(CASE WHEN cp.part_type = 'comment' AND cp.author.type IN ('admin', 'user') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id) + 1)::FLOAT AS conversation_length,
    -- Admin message count
    (LEAST(1, SUM(CASE WHEN cp.part_type = 'assignment' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id)) +
     SUM(CASE WHEN cp.part_type = 'comment' AND cp.author.type = 'admin' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id))::FLOAT AS conversation_admin_length,
    -- User message count (including initial)
    (SUM(CASE WHEN cp.part_type = 'comment' AND cp.author.type = 'user' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id) + 1)::FLOAT AS conversation_user_length,
    ic.conversation_rating.rating::INTEGER AS rating,
    -- Extract up to 6 tags
    ic.tags.tags[0].name::TEXT AS tag_1,
    ic.tags.tags[1].name::TEXT AS tag_2,
    ic.tags.tags[2].name::TEXT AS tag_3,
    ic.tags.tags[3].name::TEXT AS tag_4,
    ic.tags.tags[4].name::TEXT AS tag_5,
    ic.tags.tags[5].name::TEXT AS tag_6
  FROM public.intercom_conversations ic
  LEFT JOIN public.intercom_teams it ON it.id = ic.team_assignee_id
  LEFT JOIN public.intercom_admins ia ON ia.id = ic.admin_assignee_id
  LEFT JOIN public.intercom_conversation_parts cp ON cp.conversation_id = ic.id
  WHERE ic.custom_attributes.["Ticket category"] IS NULL
    -- Critical: Exclude imported Hiver conversations to avoid duplicates
    AND (ic.tags.tags[0].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[0].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
    AND (ic.tags.tags[1].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[1].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
    AND (ic.tags.tags[2].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[2].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
    AND (ic.tags.tags[3].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[3].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
    AND (ic.tags.tags[4].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[4].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
    AND (ic.tags.tags[5].name::TEXT IS NULL OR ic.tags.tags[5].name::TEXT <> 'ignore-for-reporting')
)
-- Get Intercom conversations (excluding imported Hiver data)
SELECT 
  id::VARCHAR,
  created_at,
  inbox_name,
  admin_name,
  time_to_admin_reply,
  time_to_first_close,
  is_closed,
  was_auto_closed,
  conversation_length,
  conversation_admin_length,
  conversation_user_length,
  rating,
  tag_1,
  tag_2,
  tag_3,
  tag_4,
  tag_5,
  tag_6
FROM intercom_conversations_filtered
WHERE rn = 1

UNION ALL

-- Add original Hiver conversations with preserved metadata
SELECT
  conversation_id::VARCHAR AS id,
  created_at::TIMESTAMP,
  CASE 
    WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '245420_hiver/Anna_Rhatigan_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Anna'
    WHEN _ab_source_file_url = '191527_hiver/Priority_l184d_new.csv' THEN 'Priority'
    ELSE SPLIT_PART(SPLIT_PART(_ab_source_file_url, '/', 2), '_', 1)
  END AS inbox_name,
  CASE WHEN first_responder = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_responder END AS admin_name, 
  CASE WHEN first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE first_response_time__business_hours___seconds_::FLOAT END AS time_to_admin_reply,
  CASE WHEN resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_ = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE resolution_time__business_hours___seconds_::FLOAT END AS time_to_first_close,
  CASE WHEN (resolved_at <> 'NA' AND resolved_at IS NOT NULL) OR (status = 'Closed' AND conversation_type <> 'Outbound') THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS is_closed,
  FALSE AS was_auto_closed,
  total_emails_count::FLOAT AS conversation_length,
  emails_sent_count::FLOAT AS conversation_admin_length,
  total_emails_count::FLOAT - emails_sent_count::FLOAT AS conversation_user_length,
  CASE 
    WHEN csat_rating = 'Not satisfied' THEN 1 
    WHEN csat_rating = 'Okay' THEN 3 
    WHEN csat_rating = 'Satisfied' THEN 5 
    ELSE NULL 
  END AS rating,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 1) END AS tag_1,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 2) END AS tag_2,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 3) END AS tag_3,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 4) END AS tag_4,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 5) END AS tag_5,
  CASE WHEN tags = 'NA' THEN NULL ELSE SPLIT_PART(tags, ',', 6) END AS tag_6
FROM public.hiver_new_conversations;

-- Step 3: Atomic swap - drop old and rename new
BEGIN;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations;
ALTER TABLE view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations_new RENAME TO view_intercom_and_hiver_conversations;

COMMIT;

- Team and admin performance metrics

- time_to_first_close: Resolution time in seconds

- conversation_admin_length: Number of admin messages
- conversation_user_length: Number of customer messages
- rating: CSAT score (1=Not satisfied, 3=Okay, 5=Satisfied)
- tag_1 through tag_6: Conversation tags

- public.intercom_conversation_parts: Message details for length calculations
- public.hiver_new_conversations: Legacy platform data (through April 2025)
- public.intercom_teams: Team/inbox information
- public.intercom_admins: Support agent information

Hiver conversations were imported into Intercom in April 2025 but tagged with 
'ignore-for-reporting' to prevent double-counting. This view:
1. Filters OUT imported Hiver data from Intercom side
2. Includes original Hiver data with complete metadata
3. Uses UNION to combine both sources without duplicates

- Excludes any conversation tagged 'ignore-for-reporting' from Intercom
- Uses row_number() to deduplicate when conversations have multiple parts

- view_intercom_conversations_with_topics: Message-level topic analysis
- Metabase dashboards: Primary consumer for support metrics

OWNER: Data Team

- conversation_admin_length: Number of admin messages
- conversation_user_length: Number of customer messages
- rating: CSAT score (1=Not satisfied, 3=Okay, 5=Satisfied)
- tag_1 through tag_6: Conversation tags

WITH all_parts AS (
  -- Include all comment parts from conversation_parts
  SELECT
    cp.id AS part_id,
    cp.body,
    cp.author,
    cp.conversation_id,
    cp.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags,
    c.team_assignee_id,
    c.admin_assignee_id,
    c.statistics,
    c.state,
    c.conversation_rating
  FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
  JOIN public.intercom_conversations c ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT)
  WHERE cp.part_type = 'comment'

  UNION ALL

  -- Include initial conversation messages (or placeholder) for conversations without comment parts
  -- This ensures ALL conversations are represented, even those with no messages
  SELECT
    CONCAT('conv_', c.id) AS part_id,  -- Create unique ID for initial message
    COALESCE(c.conversation_message.body::VARCHAR, '') AS body,  -- Use empty string if NULL
    c.conversation_message.author AS author,
    CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) AS conversation_id,
    c.created_at AS part_created_at,
    c.created_at AS conversation_created_at,
    c.tags,
    c.team_assignee_id,
    c.admin_assignee_id,
    c.statistics,
    c.state,
    c.conversation_rating
  FROM public.intercom_conversations c
  WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp2 
    WHERE cp2.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
    AND cp2.part_type = 'comment'
  )
)
SELECT
  part_id,

content.text:
These are conversations that exist but have NO comment parts. They appear to be:
- Email conversations that were created but never got any replies/comments
- They have NULL message bodies and subjects
- Mix of open and closed states
- All from email source

Here's a query you can run to explore more:

```sql
-- Count conversations with and without parts
SELECT 
  CASE 
    WHEN cp.id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Has comment parts'
    ELSE 'No comment parts'
  END as has_parts,
  COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) as conversation_count,
  MIN(TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + c.created_at * INTERVAL '1 second') as earliest,
  MAX(TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + c.created_at * INTERVAL '1 second') as latest
FROM public.intercom_conversations c
LEFT JOIN public.intercom_conversation_parts cp 
  ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
  AND cp.part_type = 'comment'
GROUP BY 1;
```

These "empty" conversations would be missed by the current `view_cx_conversations` query because the LEFT JOIN wouldn't produce any rows for them to be counted.

message.content: i guess are you saying that there are some conversations in intercom_conversation_parts that are NOT in intercom_conversations, and therefore we have a lower count for intercom_conversations?

content.text:
No, it's the opposite! There are conversations in `intercom_conversations` that have NO entries in `intercom_conversation_parts`. 

Let me clarify with a count:

input.sql_query:
SELECT 
  COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) as total_conversations,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN cp.id IS NOT NULL THEN c.id END) as conversations_with_parts,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN cp.id IS NULL THEN c.id END) as conversations_without_parts
FROM public.intercom_conversations c
LEFT JOIN public.intercom_conversation_parts cp 
  ON cp.conversation_id = CAST(c.id AS BIGINT) 
  AND cp.part_type = 'comment'
WHERE c.custom_attributes.["Ticket category"] IS NULL
  AND (c.tags.tags[0].name::text IS NULL OR c.tags.tags[0].name::text <> 'ignore-for-reporting')

content.text:
Wow! Out of 342,125 Intercom conversations:
- **194,638 have comment parts** (messages)
- **147,487 have NO comment parts** (empty conversations)

The issue with `view_cx_conversations` is that it does a LEFT JOIN between conversations and parts, but then uses `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ic.id)`. For conversations WITHOUT parts:
- The LEFT JOIN produces NO rows (not even a NULL row)
- So these 147,487 conversations never appear in the result set

That's why we need to handle them separately, like we did in `view_intercom_conversations_with_topics` where we:
1. Get all conversations WITH parts
2. UNION with conversations WITHOUT parts

Should I fix `view_cx_conversations` to include all 342,125 conversations?

message.content: If you look at the part type, i bet it's because the intercom conversation parts aren't conversationsa at all!

content.text: You're absolutely right! Let me check what part_types exist:

input.sql_query:
SELECT 
  cp.part_type,
  COUNT(*) as part_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT cp.conversation_id) as conversation_count
FROM public.intercom_conversation_parts cp
GROUP BY cp.part_type
ORDER BY part_count DESC

content.text:
Exactly! Most part_types are NOT actual messages:
- Only **"comment"** parts are actual conversation messages (284,966 parts across 208,882 conversations)
- Everything else is metadata/events: assignments, closes, tag updates, attribute changes, etc.

So when we filter for `part_type = 'comment'`:
- We only get 208,882 conversations that have actual comments
- We miss ~133,000 conversations that exist but have no comments yet

These conversations without comments might be:
- Just created but no one has replied yet
- Auto-created from emails that haven't been responded to
- Closed without any comments

The current `view_cx_conversations` query uses a LEFT JOIN on ALL parts (not filtered to comments), which is why it would capture more conversations. But it's counting assignment events, closes, etc. as "parts" for the conversation length calculations.
